title: 'Fast X' and the Desecration of Movies. Plus: Paul Schrader!
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins take apart 'Fast X' and what franchise fatigue is doing to movies, then Sean talks to Paul Schrader about 'Master Gardener' and the Lonely Man trilogy.

segments.heading: 'Fast X' and the desecration of movies

films.title: Fast X
films.note: The 11th 'Fast' film, directed by Louis Leterrier after Justin Lin left mid-production. Opens by re-editing the 'Fast Five' vault heist to insert Jason Momoa's villain, Dante, son of Hernan Reyes; crams in nearly every returning star and ends on a dam cliffhanger Sean calls the most cynical thing he's ever seen. Amanda flags Brie Larson's exposition and mismatched eyebrows; both liked Alan Ritchson.

films.title: Fast Five
films.note: Sean's high-water mark for the series and one of his favorite films of its decade: the Rio vault heist with Vin Diesel and Paul Walker. Fast X leans on it and, Sean argues, only reminds you how far things have fallen.

films.title: Furious 7
films.note: Paired with 'Fast Five' as the other peak, for how it closed out Paul Walker's story after his death.

films.title: The Fate of the Furious
films.note: The eighth film, badly reviewed but still a box-office hit. Where Amanda marks the decline setting in.

films.title: F9
films.note: The one where the characters go to space; cited as the previous ceiling on escalating stakes that Fast X fails to top.

films.title: Master Gardener
films.note: Schrader writes and directs Joel Edgerton as a horticulturist with a violent past on a Southern estate owned by Sigourney Weaver's character. The tonal inverse of Fast X and, for Sean, a hopeful capstone to a trilogy that asks whether a former Proud Boy can be forgiven and loved.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: The 2018 release Sean calls one of the most significant films of his life; the trilogy's opener, with Ethan Hawke as a minister undone by climate grief.

films.title: The Card Counter
films.note: The middle film, Oscar Isaac as a poker player and former Abu Ghraib torturer.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: Ground zero for his sensibility: a lonely man drawn from Dostoevsky, Sartre and Camus that he says caught the zeitgeist. He returns to the Peter Boyle diner scene, where an older man gives Travis the worst advice, as the pattern his late films reverse.

films.title: Light Sleeper
films.note: His midlife-crisis film, the drug-delivery-boy 'John' who appeared to him in a 5 a.m. dream and gave him his metaphor.

films.title: Hardcore
films.note: The ending he most regrets: Columbia forced a daughter rescue where he wanted her to die unrelated to the pornography her father chased. The theater scene is now a meme.

films.title: American Gigolo
films.note: 'The gigolo' in his run of occupational metaphors.

films.title: Cat People
films.note: One of the films that departs from the lonely-man mold.

films.title: Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters
films.note: Another departure; he's gratified it still screens, and that you can hear Philip Glass's score in an elevator.

films.title: The Comfort of Strangers
films.note: Cited as a film a Criterion reissue introduced to a new generation.

films.title: Patty Hearst
films.note: One he keeps badgering Criterion to revisit.

films.title: Adam Resurrected
films.note: Among his least-seen; he admits he didn't quite get the ending right.

films.title: Rolling Thunder
films.note: Quentin Tarantino told him he'd like to refilm its ending for a project about 1970s Hollywood.

films.title: Beau Is Afraid
films.note: His answer to the show's closing question: not great, maybe not even good, but a force of imagination that stayed in his head 'like this very cute girl that you just want to slap.'
